sentences.text: Going back a little bit, to this day I-- I-- I think it's-- it's so amazing that my parents were influenced and convinced to let go of their children, which is such-- it must be so hard for a mother to do.
Because I know we were the only ones from-- from that-- that-- I don't know how many families that were there.
Just my brother, and I, and that little girl were the only ones that-- that went.
The other parents, I guess just said no way, no how, that they would not let their kids go.
And I'm-- 9 out of 10, they're not around anymore.
So.
So it was just a miracle.
I'm sorry, what?
It's a-- it's amazing that they were convinced, yeah.
Yeah.
I don't-- like-- like I said, I don't recall anything.
So they must have talked, either in Czech or Hungarian, which I-- [LAUGHS] which I didn't speak.
Yeah, we thought we were going for a trip-- for a-- for-- like a field trip, you know.
We went-- went on one a few days before that.
And I thought that's where we were going again.
[LAUGHS]
That was it.
I mean, we left.
And [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] as we were driving off in-- in that-- that car, black car-- I keep saying this.
And I guess I'm too lazy to find out the exact date.
It's easy to find.
It's-- it's a historic day.
It was the day the Germans just moved in-- into Prague.
Because I-- I remember this trip, that all of a sudden, we came to a traffic jam.
And we were right in the midst of all this traffic of the-- of the German military.
They-- they just-- there was not one shot fired.
I mean, they just moved right in and took over Prague.
This didn't happen right away.
In fact, I-- I remember the frustration of first stopping, which is-- and then nothing happening.
We'd just sit, and sit, and sit, which seemed like forever-- and getting kind of stressed out.
Why are we not moving?
And then slowly, we heard the noise of oncoming commotion, of-- of voices.
And-- and then what it was, it was the Czechs.
The-- the civilians were lined in the street like a parade.
But they were not cheering.
They were screaming and crying.
And then I saw, eventually, the German military coming in their vehicles.
I remember the-- the motorcycles with the sidecars, with the shining helmets, and the smiling Germans.
And there was-- there was the contrast between the-- them smiling and the-- the civilians who were screaming and crying, and you know.
That was-- I remember that.
That was kind of significant-- and of course, then, the-- the trucks, the open trucks with the German soldiers on top.
So-- so that was why we had to wait all that time, I guess-- because of the congestion that that was causing.
We-- we were going to Prague the same time they were going to Prague.
Because we were on the way to Prague, yeah.
So whatever day they took Prague, that's the day I left my parents.
Exactly that day.
Yeah.
All they-- all she said was-- my father was in the background, I don't remember.
I don't have recollection.
But she had said, do you sure you want to go?
That's what I remember her asking.
And we said, sure, why not?
You know, why-- why the fuss?
Why is she crying?
[LAUGHS]
So.
[SIGHS] Probably nothing.
What is there to take?
I mean, you know-- [LAUGHS] wardrobe?
We were, you know--
--poor.
No.
I don't remember taking anything.
Probably.
I don't recall--
--anything else.
I don't remember carrying anything, or-- no.
No.
Never can--
What?
No, no, no, no, no.
I-- I remember [INAUDIBLE] I remember like-- we were taken to a-- what seemed like a multi-story building, you know.
You know, for a kid, a two-story is already a big building, you know.
And we were brought together with other small children to some location there.
And there were a couple-- two or three-- older girls who kind of watched over us.
They were probably in their teens, you know, around 16, 17, 18.
I don't know.
In fact, one of these girls happened to have been a sister to one of the kids, that we later on, were together in-- in this boy's home.
His name was Sam Brown.
And his sister, Frieda Brown, was one of the girls that was watching over us.
And I met her later on in England.
Anyway, my memories from that place-- what can I say?
I left my parents.
We just had celebrated Purim, when my father dressed up.
And-- so the next Jewish festival is Passover.
And I remember being sent to Jewish families to celebrate it, you know, the feast, which is two nights.
I don't remember if we were sent two nights, but I do remember celebrating with-- with families.
Where was this?
Do you remember?
In Prague.
In Prague, yeah.
[SIGHS] I don't know.
I-- I-- the memories I have of Prague are kind of spotty.
One thing I remember, there was Germans everywhere.
For some reason, I always remember wherever you looked, there were these shining helmets, and rifles.
And they were always dressed you know, real sharp-- and rifles, always with a bayonets on 'em-- kind of scary, you know?
Not that they bothered us directly, but they really had a threatening-- the environment was kind of threatening.
And-- and it seems like every store had a German in front of it.
And I have a feeling they were kind of like-- they walked in the stores and just-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, nobody stopped them from doing what they wanted to do kind of thing.
And-- but there still wasn't the war with England.
I mean, they just took over Prague.
And they just took-- like I said-- took over just about everything.
Wherever you-- you looked, there was Germans, and their trucks drove by many times.
And you'd see 'em all the time.
Is that my pager or yours?
OK.
Oh, now that you mention it, yeah.
Yeah.
We had like, in-- in the-- that location, where we were at, it was not a restricted, like, like, you know, earlier, with my parents.
It was just we were under the, you know, we were being watched-- taken care of by these teenagers, [LAUGHS] I guess.
I remember doing crafts.
In fact, now that you mention it, one of the crafts-- I'm glad you brought that up.
Boy, that's something I really haven't even thought about in a long [LAUGHS] time.
Not that it's significant.
But I do remember one of the things we did was sewing.
Like-- like on cardboard, we'd sew designs-- pictures, you know-- you know-- like-- [LAUGHS]
I remember doing that.
Yeah, it's crafts, you know, like we'd-- we'd sew pictures on-- on-- on thin cardboard, something like that.
Yeah, I remember doing that as-- as a craft.
Oh boy.
I don't think there was that many in that location.
I don't have a feeling that-- like, it was overwhelming, as far as many.
Yeah, something like that.
Yeah.
More like that.
Probably very similar, because the teenagers were, like, much older than us.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
Yeah.
So they probably-- it wouldn't-- year two, that age-- age range.
OK.
We were there, like I said, till after Passover.
Because I remember Passover.
And I remember-- [SIGHS] God, you know, thinking back, I was always protective of my brother, you know?
And-- because he was-- he-- as a child, he-- he wasn't well, even though he was older than me.
And I was more worried about how-- the way he was feeling than the way I was feeling.
Although, you know, I missed my parents.
And I always wondered, when are we going to see our parents?
When are we going to-- so he's the one that always used to ask that question.
When are we going to see our parents?
When are we going to see our parents?
And I don't know what they told us.
[LAUGHS] Oh, you don't recall what they would say.
But but they always said, yeah, you know.
It won't be much longer.
I don't know.
What else can they tell us?
Eventually, we were taken from this place-- I'm sure by train-- to Holland.
And that's how we-- from Holland to England-- and that's how we got to England there.
You know something?
I don't remember it.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, maybe, as a kid, I was tired and fell asleep or something.
And-- the boat, I don't even remember the boat that significantly.
No.
I don't know.
But I know we-- we-- we got to Calais from-- from Holland.
We landed at Calais, and then Liverpool Street Station.
I mean, that's-- I mean, that's documented I-- I'm sure.
So that's how we got to England.
[SIGHS] God, they must have.
You know, I've been wondering about that.
Because we were-- we were hurting.
You know, kids, to be without their parents, at that age, for a long period, they keep asking, and asking, and asking.
And-- God.
I don't remember what they told me, to be honest with you.
They must have given me some kind of stall, you know.
Or it won't be much longer, or-- why I was going to England?
I didn't understand why I was going-- no idea why I was going.
And your brother doesn't remember either.
Yeah.
And there we were taken to a-- to a home, a-- a rabbi's home, which was just a short time.
And I think what happened is shortly after that-- we were several children, I think, in the rabbi's home. and-- oh, we even started school there.
We-- we were enrolled in-- in-- in-- in East London Jewish Free School.
It was-- it was a-- it was a pretty large school.
I have very-- not too much memory from that school, because I think shortly after that, war broke out.
And the British, right away, they-- they're great about organizing stuff.
They-- they organized all these evacuation-- you know, we-- we were evacuated to up north-- foster homes-- in-- God, I don't know.
Hundreds and hundreds of kids, we all had tags so we don't get lost-- [LAUGHS]
--with our name on-- [LAUGHS] our names and stuff.
That's the first time I tasted cornflakes.
[LAUGHS]
I don't know.
It's somewhere en route.
And I said, gee, that's kind of different, you know?
You know, coming from a kosher background, anything that wasn't-- not that it's-- that it's not kosher, I mean, it's just cornflakes.
But it-- it-- it was like a suspect, you know?
[LAUGHS] But I remember it was a-- it was different.
Anyway.
Yeah, so here we were, all these hundreds of kids.
I don't know how they kept track of us.
Yeah, we all had these names.
And we were shipped-- taken by train by-- up north.
And I remember they-- they put us in with a-- a family that was almost on top of railroad tracks.
[LAUGHS] Yeah.
It was a temporary thing.
Oh!
Yeah!
[LAUGHS] I mean, it was like I could almost touch the train.
I mean, it was right on the tracks almost.
In fact, I remember it was a village.
It was-- they got-- they have some crazy names in England.
It was called Shippea Hill.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
[LAUGHS] Yeah, it's a small little village in North England, probably in Cambridgeshire-- or 'shear'.
Because we ended up in Ely-- E-L-Y-- which is a small town-- a small city-- 8,000 people it had.
And we-- we-- and they put us in a boys' home-- a Jewish boys' home.
And we stayed there for the duration of the war.
To-- to '46, I think, after the war.
Yeah.
Well, in the home.
It varied.
I think we were as high as 60, maybe.
It was a huge house.
Ely was like country.
And I guess real estate wasn't that expensive.
We had this-- they gave us, as a residence, this old house.
[LAUGHS] It was four stories high.
[LAUGHS]
I describe this house to people.
It blows their mind.
It had two front entrances, because it was so wide and big.
It had two entrances, OK?
I try to count the rooms.
I think it was about 40.
[LAUGHS] It had a basement plus four stories.
And we had acres of land in the back.
You'd think it was a paradise.
Well, trust me, after a couple of years, I was so bored over [LAUGHS] there.
And-- and with the city, Ely, like, I used to say, I know every brick in this place.
And--
Oh, yeah.
Well, the school was-- well, the Jewish Free School was a very big school.
So they broke it up into sections.
And a section was-- was evacuated-- I mean, was-- re--
--located to-- to Ely.
And we were given the-- a part of a school that was called, I believe--
--King School.
Yeah, it was King School.
And Ely was like the most prestigious school.
I mean, the whole city only had maybe three schools.
But [LAUGHS] that was the most prestigious one.
And they had this-- this old buildings-- God, hundreds of years old-- like castle-looking type thing.
It's a great place for tourism.
But to live there, you know, it was boring.
And they gave us one section.
It was called Hereward Hall-- H-E-R-- Her-- H-E-R-E-W-A-R-D, Hall-- Hereward Hall-- and the building.
And it was a big, brick building.
And that was where the Jewish Free School relocated.
And Hereward Hall was named after Hereward the Wake, [LAUGHS] which is, in English history, God-- going back to-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] probably before 10,000 even-- way back there.
He was supposed to-- Ely has a very, very historic background.
There's a-- a hill there.
It's called Hereward Hill.
He was like a-- a rebel.
And he was always being sought by the authorities.
And he would hide in that hill.
And Oliver Cromwell is from Ely.
It has a cathedral that's-- that's supposed to be the-- it's not the largest.
It's the tallest cathedral in England, I think.
It's built hundreds of years ago.
And I-- it's so fascinating to see how hundreds of years ago, they're-- they're able to build something with such accuracy.
And, I mean, it's like symmetric, and perfect, and huge.
They didn't have any equipment in those days.
And it's unbelievable.
Anyway, it still stands there.
And opposite the cathedral is this house that Oliver Cromwell lived in.
He was a Puritan that-- that took power from King Charles, I believe-- you know, if you know anything about English history.
And-- and when he took over, because of his religious beliefs, he smashed all the-- the beautiful pane-- windows-- glass-- in the cathedral, which has been replaced since.
Anyway, his house, we-- we were invited for tea there as-- as kids, several times, by the vicar.
It's a-- his house is-- is the permanent residence of the Vicar of Ely, which is like, the-- he's the highest--
--Christian, whatever, you know.
So--
And he had these nice church boys over to--
Yeah, he invited us.
In fact, it wasn't him.
His wife, Mrs.
Knowles-- very, very lovely lady-- she would invite us for tea there, and a couple of times, yeah, we went there.
[SIGHS] Well, we--
--visited a good-- no.
This wasn't-- this was outside the-- the, you know.
Oliver Cromwell's house was a residence of the vicar, that was opposite-- across from the-- from-- there.
There was like a-- a-- a big grass lawn-- a huge area-- I don't know, a few acres.
And beyond that was-- outside like, the gate was-- was the building for-- the residence.
No, no.
Jewish Free School was totally non-religious.
It was just a Jewish school.
That's all it was.
Although, in Ely, we had gentiles going there also.
Because it wasn't just for refugees from Europe, no.
Jewish Free School was for Jewish people, so for Jewish kids.
--what?
[SIGHS] Well, hearing, yeah.
We used to have air raids [LAUGHS] frequently in Ely.
You know, we used to hear, you know, the-- the sirens going at night.
And we'd have to go get out of bed.
And we go downstairs, into-- to the-- into the ground floor.
And we would sit underneath.
Usually, there was like a staircase, which I guess they thought that was the safest place.
And we'd sit there for the duration of the air raid.
And I remember--
--how my stomach used to go, when we used to hear planes overhead.
Because it was silent, OK?
And then you'd hear a plane coming.
And you'd wonder, is it a-- is it ours or-- or somebody else's, you know?
And so--
[SIGHS] Not that much.
The thing is that Ely, even though we were north, we had a-- an air field not far from Ely.
We used to go visit it sometimes-- walk.
It was a few miles.
We used to go visit over there and pick blackberries as kids.
But they had these four-- four-engine bombers that used to-- used to, you know, go on missions almost every night to Germany.
So I think one of the biggest airfields was right near Ely.
So I don't know if that was significant or what.
But I wouldn't say it was a lot, those air raids.
But it was enough to-- to-- to get you-- kinda-- I remember my stomach used to go like that.
And then of course, you'd hear these bombs go off.
And sometimes it would shake like an earthquake.
But-- yeah.
Always asked-- always asked.
My brother was the one who used to ask a lot.
I used to always worry more about him.
Hopefully soon, you know.
You know.
But, you know, the older we got, and the more we realized what was going on.
Like when-- when the war was over, '45-- how old was I? 12-- right?
We were old enough to understand from the information that was coming out with all the-- you know, concentration camps, and all that.
And we hadn't had any mail since 19-- the last time we got mail was in '43.
And it was a-- a letter that was late in coming.
I think it was mailed in '41-- two years later.
I have no idea how that letter finally got to us.
And that's when we heard that we had another brother.
That was--
Yeah, yeah.
From the-- from the Jewish ghetto in Bratislava-- yeah, from Judengasse, it was called.
Judengasse means Jewish street.
That's German for-- I don't member the address, but I remember it was-- came from Judengasse, in Bratislava, which is the Jewish ghetto.
But that's where my-- my parents were.
Well, I couldn't read in those days, Czech or-- it was-- probably was in Yiddish.
So one of the older kids kind of read it to me.
I think his name was Alex Singer-- I still remember.
And I don't remember what that letter said, except that we had another brother.
That was the only thing that was significant.
And his name was Irwin.
And he was born in '41, I guess.
And-- that was the last letter we got from my parents, yeah, so-- you know, when the war ended.
You know, there was always this hoping, anticipating that we'll-- we'll-- we'll finally meet-- meet-- meet our parents.
And we saw that kind of fading, you know, as a possibility.
[SIGHS] Well, we never really found out ever, exactly what happened.

It was only from the relatives that we met in the United States that just acknowledged that they were taken from the ghetto one-- at one time, probably in the late, you know, the latter part of the-- of the war, out of the ghetto, into concentration camps, and never came out.
So--
I don't remember-- not a whole lot.
I don't-- in fact, that's the only letter I could remember.
Well, there might have been a couple of others.
But I guess the post in those days wasn't the greatest, you know, especially coming out of a ghetto.
I mean, if that came from, like, from a major city, Prague, even then, it was war time.
You know, it's-- I don't know.
There might have been a couple of letters before that.
I know there-- there weren't a lot.
No.
Eh, they probably-- what they said, you know, I hope you have your-- you know, you're both well, and that's about it.
I mean, what else?
You know.
[SIGHS] I don't remember writing back ever.
No.
I'll never remember.
Little kids couldn't even write.
[LAUGHS]
I mean, I learned English at the age of six, you know?
We went in-- I went straight into first grade.
And before long, I guess I could write English.
But-- Did you get a good education over there?
Oh yes, absolutely.
In England, you get a great education, yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Well--
--not that much.
Not-- we didn't really talk about that a whole lot.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I think maybe one-- one of those kids I know, mother survived.
But most of them, I don't think-- they were probably in the same position we were.
In fact, I was watching a video last night, which I hadn't seen in a few years.
When I went to the 50th year reunion, and the stories that people were telling were-- were so much like mine.
It was like-- it was that-- they were told when-- because they came with-- with children transports, like I did.
And they were told that the parents were supposed to follow.
But the war broke out, and it didn't happen.
And-- and-- and they ended up in-- in concentration camps.
And that was the last they heard.
It's exactly what happened to my parents.
So that was probably pretty much common what was-- what-- what happened to most--
Yeah.
Oh, yeah.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, a little bit.
Absolutely.
Yeah.
Anyway, we were in Ely for-- till the end of the war.
And we were probably one of the last people that went back to London.
Because most of the kids who had-- because in Ely, we-- we were not refugees, we were evacuees.
Oh, can I check that page, please?
